articles.title: About me
articles.description: Hi there! I'm Alex. I'm a postdoctoral fellow in Neuroscience and this is my personal website.
articles.contents:
I’m a neuroscientist in the Washington, DC area. I received my PhD in Neuroscience at the University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus in Denver, CO. I’m now a postdoctoral fellow at the NIH, and on the side I am pursuing part-time studies in Statistics.
My past and present research is described on my Research page.
I also really like running, taking pictures of my cat, painting and scientific illustration, and board games. I built this website with the R package {Distill}, which you can learn more about here.

articles.title: Cauchy & Misc
articles.description: Yes, welcome, this is a whole page of miscellanea (mostly my cat).
articles.contents:
Other links
Running log
Amateur art
Cauchy
Cauchy’s response to “Foods?”This is Cauchy, named for this guy. I adopted him as a kitten from a shelter on my birthday several years ago. Words Cauchy currently responds to are his name (and also variants “Cauch” and “Mr. Cauch”), “foods”, “treats”, and “drugs” (catnip).
Sometimes he gets haircuts!Sometimes he plays games!Usually he interrupts work.

articles.title: Running log
articles.description: DC is a lot hillier than Denver!
articles.contents:
Contents
Miles
Racing

Fall colors on 7th St in the Congress Park neighborhood.Running is an outlet and it’s important to me that I keep it fun. I don’t wear a watch (prefer perceived effort) and instead map distances post-run with Footpath, which I highly recommend.
Footpath map of a good long run around Washington Park in Denver.Miles
I started tracking weekly distances mid-2018, so I now have two full years of run data (2019, 2020). A neat thing about building this site with R is that I can easily show and plot these data. Here are some weekly data:

Show code
library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)
library(kableExtra)

theme_set(theme_cowplot(font_size =18))




Show code
running <- read_csv('annualmpw.csv')
showrun <- as.data.frame(running) 




Show code
#Fantastic html tables with kableExtra, 
#check out https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/kableExtra/vignettes/awesome_table_in_html.html

kbl(showrun) %>%
  kable_paper()%>%
  scroll_box(height = "120px")

And plotted. I’m surprised at how similar my annual total was between 2019 and 2020, since I waited until the end of the year to check the total and I was pretty sure I cut back a little bit this year (can sort of see it around week 25?).

Show code
running_long <- running %>% 
  pivot_longer(!week, names_to = "year", values_to = "miles")




Show code
ggplot(running_long, aes(x=week, y=miles, color=year))+
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#fc7b6d", "#05354b", "#008080"))+
  geom_line(span=0.2, na.rm=TRUE)+
  geom_point(size=3, alpha=0.6, na.rm=TRUE)+
  theme(legend.position = "top")+
  labs(x="week 1 - 52", y="miles/week") 





Show code
annual_miles <- running %>%
  summarize(`total 2019` = sum(miles_2019), 
            `total 2020` = sum(miles_2020),
            `YTD` = sum(miles_2021, na.rm=TRUE))

as.data.frame(annual_miles) %>%
  kbl() %>%
  kable_paper()



total 2019


total 2020


YTD


2044.77


2026.18


1311.46


Racing
I don’t race often (and most races were canceled last year). Still, I have a hard time keeping track of the outcomes when I do race, so I’m keeping PRs here.
5k PR: 20:15 (6:53 min/mi), 5/4/2019 National MS Society 5K at City Park, Denver, CO. 1st F / 4th overall.
10k PR: 42:39 (6:52 min/mi), 12/7/2019 Santa Stampede in Littleton, CO.
10 mile: 1:13:49 (7:23 min/mi), 1/25/2019 Frosty’s Frozen 5 and 10 at Hudson Gardens in Littleton, CO. 4th F.
Half marathon: 1:36:05 (7:20 min/mi), 2/22/2020 Snowman Stampede at Hudson Gardens in Littleton, CO. 4th F.
Full marathon? TBD!
National MS Society 5k, May 2019

articles.title: Gallery
articles.description: I started painting more regularly at the onset of the pandemic and am still very much an amateur. Most of these are acrylic on stretched canvas, unless otherwise noted. Some are available in my Redbubble shop!
articles.contents:
Contents
Zebrafish art
Microglia art
Oligodendrocyte art
Not science art

Redbubble shop
My partner and I had covid-19 a couple weeks before it was reported that cats could get sick, too.
Cauchy screams (or yawns)
Zebrafish art
The zebrafish spinal cord, viewed laterally but imagined underwater, complete with octodendrocytes and a seafloor plant Mauthner.
Zebrafish embryos in chorions!Adult zebrafishCan you spot the zebrafish? A Denver zebrafish starry night. 
Geometric zebrafish bbsMicroglia art
Tectal microglia, here isolated (n=9 cells from each of 9 larvae) and below hugging neuronal somas. 
Tectal microglia and neurons!Oligodendrocyte art
Bought a clock kit to timelapse-image oligodendrocytes at home! A sheath per hour is about 2.4 fold slower than this occurs in fish (5 hours for all sheaths). It’s always oligo’clock! 
Making more functional things. These are glia coasters (L to R): NG2, radial glia, microglia, oligodendrocyte. 
When I started writing my dissertation (esp acknowledgments!) it was evident that I was going to need tissues. 
Oligodendrocytes without axons (first attempt)
Oligodendrocytes and axons, playing with depth (much improved)
Not science art
FlowersTrianglesA little too much time to reminisce during WFH. Lucy and Pearl, my pet birds (cockatiels) that I had as a kid. 
I cut a couple square canvases and painted them as Zoom frames for a very 2020 Halloween.

articles.title: Reading
articles.contents:
Distill is a publication format for scientific and technical writing, native to the web.
Learn more about using Distill for R Markdown at https://rstudio.github.io/distill.

articles.title: Research & Publications
articles.description: Past and present research in neuroscience and developmental biology.
articles.contents:
Contents
Overview
Graduate research
Previous work & undergraduate research

Overview
I use zebrafish to investigate development and function of glial cells of the central nervous system. Zebrafish are small, transparent vertebrates that develop quickly. Here is one at 24 hours post-fertilization (hpf). I’ve labeled the heart with green fluorescent protein.
Zebrafish at 24 hpf.Timeline of glial and neuronal development in zebrafish.Graduate research
I did my graduate training in Bruce Appel’s lab at the University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus and defended my PhD in Neuroscience in September 2020. I pursued two separate projects in the lab: in the first, I investigated the localization and function of synaptic proteins in oligodendrocytes, and in the second I brought microglia into the lab and discovered that microglia prune myelin sheaths during development.
Hughes AN & Appel B (2019). Oligodendrocytes express synaptic proteins that modulate myelin sheath formation. Nature Communications, 10(1), 1-15.
Paper link
PDF

Hughes AN & Appel B (2020). Microglia phagocytose myelin sheaths to modify developmental myelination. Nature Neuroscience, 23, 1055–1066.
Paper link
PDF

Hughes AN (2021). Glial cells promote myelin formation and elimination. Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology, 9, 661486.
Paper link
PDF

I had the opportunity to write a blog piece for The Node about the story behind the microglia story, Retracting sheaths & words
Open Box Science invited to me give a presentation on the microglia paper, which is available to view on Youtube
My dissertation
Microglia exhibit frequent calcium transients during development.Microglia in the larval optic tectum.Previous work & undergraduate research
During my first year of graduate school, I wrote a journal club-style review about the relationship between Schwann cells and motor neuron axon arborization with my peers Alison Hixon and Megan Josey.
Hughes AN, Hixon AM, Josey M (2016). A Schwanncentric View of Axon Arborization in Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ) Formation. Journal of Neuroscience, 36(38), 9760-9762.
PDF

I performed undergraduate research in Julie Oxford’s lab at Boise State University. Julie’s lab studies collagens, a family of extracellular matrix proteins produced by chondrocytes during long bone development. My research project investigated how the minor fibrillar collagen chain encoded by Col11a1 functions in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) of chondrocytes during development. During my time in the lab, I wrote two reviews about ER unfolded protein response (UPR) signaling, one in the context of bone development and the other in the context of liver development (with consultation from Dr. Kristen Mitchell, also at BSU).
Hughes AN, Oxford A, Tawara K, Jorcyk C, Oxford JT (2017). Endoplasmic reticulum stress and unfolded protein response in cartilage pathophysiology; contributing factors to apoptosis and osteoarthritis. International Journal of Molecular Sciences, 18(3), 665.
PDF

Hughes AN & Oxford JT (2014). A lipid-rich gestational diet predisposes offspring to nonalcoholic fatty liver disease. Hepatic Medicine, 6, 15-23.
PDF
